category_title: Child with ASD, ADHD, and Specific Learning Disability exhibiting academic resistance and behavioral outbursts
minimal_context: Sam and his tutor are sitting in a quiet spot for a one-to-one lesson to practice identifying letter sounds.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Let's look at the letter we learned this morning. Tell me what sound it makes.

STUDENT: I don't want to read.

TUTOR: Just try your best to remember the sound.

STUDENT: I don't know it!

TUTOR: Okay, let's look at this new letter instead. What sound do you think this one makes?

STUDENT: (Pushes the letters off the table and begins to scream) No! I'm not doing it!

options.A: Provide one-to-one instruction in a quiet spot with minimal auditory or visual distractions, keeping initial lessons to five to ten minutes in duration but occurring several times a day.
options.B: Use instruments as figurative media, such as images, posters, toys (balls, blocks), and others, during the one-to-one instructional sessions for identifying letter sounds.
options.C: Ask open-ended questions so that the student can write about what he knows regarding the letter sounds during the one-to-one instructional sessions throughout the day.
options.D: Encourage the student to bring in items from home that he considers special using 'The Important Bag' activity during the one-to-one instructional sessions for identifying letter sounds.
